one_line_takeaway: QPCert is the first white-box poisoning/backdoor certificate for (graph) neural networks, reformulating the poisoning bilevel problem over an NTK-equivalent SVM as a MILP, and it both certifies non-trivial robustness on Cora-ML/WikiCS and reveals that graph connectivity provably boosts worst-case robustness over an MLP.
key_finding: QPCert yields non-trivial certificates and turns the certificate into an analysis tool. (1) Even when the adversary can poison ALL labeled nodes (padv=1 in the PL setting), where a trivial certificate gives 0% certified accuracy, QPCert certifies a substantial fraction on Cora-MLb and WikiCSb. (2) Across all datasets and settings, every GNN has markedly higher certified accuracy than an MLP, showing that leveraging graph connectivity provably improves worst-case poisoning robustness (in the PU setting the MLP training is unaffected by the adversary, making it a clean baseline that GNNs still beat). (3) Architectural insights emerge: GIN and GraphSAGE give the lowest certified accuracy of all GNNs, tied to their sum-aggregation; SGC's high provable robustness may partly reflect its tighter certificate. Graph connectivity shows an inflection at delta=0.05 (higher connectivity helps for small perturbations but hurts for strong ones), and APPNP's teleport parameter alpha shows an analogous inflection at delta=0.1, unique to the PU setting. (4) The certificate is provably deterministic for infinite-width NNs / kernelized SVMs and only becomes a high-probability guarantee at finite width, qualitatively stronger than randomized-smoothing certificates.
core_contribution: The paper introduces QPCert, the first white-box certificate against data-poisoning and (clean-label) backdoor attacks for neural networks, instantiated for Graph Neural Networks in semi-supervised node classification. The key idea is to exploit the equivalence (Chen et al., 2021) between an infinitely-wide NN trained with the soft-margin loss and a soft-margin SVM using the network's Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) as its kernel. Poisoning is a bilevel optimization problem (adversary perturbs training data in the upper level; model is retrained in the lower level); the authors reformulate this bilevel problem for kernelized SVMs into a single-level Mixed-Integer Linear Program (MILP) by (a) replacing the lower-level SVM with its KKT conditions, (b) linearizing complementary-slackness via big-M constraints with provably valid, tight big-M values (Prop. 3.2), and (c) linearizing the remaining bilinear alpha*Q terms using derived element-wise lower/upper bounds on the (perturbed) NTK matrix. A test node is certified robust if the MILP optimum is > 0. They derive closed-form NTKs and their perturbation bounds for GCN, SGC, APPNP, GIN, GraphSAGE and MLP (deriving APPNP/GIN/GraphSAGE NTKs themselves), and prove the NTK bounds are tight for linear-activation models (SGC, (A)PPNP, linear MLP; Thm 3.5). The framework generalizes beyond graphs to any NN with an NTK equivalence and to any kernelized SVM.
threat_model: Training-time data poisoning and backdoor attacks on node features. The adversary controls the features of an epsilon-fraction of nodes (the 'unverified'/untrusted set U), following the semi-verified learning setup of Charikar et al. (2017) in which k known-clean 'verified' nodes exist. Each controlled node's features can be perturbed within an lp-ball B_p(x) of radius delta (p in {1,2,inf}), i.e. a bounded clean-label threat model (features only; labels unchanged). For pure poisoning the adversary maximizes test-node misclassification; for backdoors the adversary additionally perturbs the target test node at test time and only needs to flip that controlled node. Four evaluation settings: Poison Labeled (PL), Poison Unlabeled (PU), Backdoor Labeled (BL), Backdoor Unlabeled (BU). Structure/edge perturbations and label-flipping are explicitly out of scope.
limitations: Tightness: the element-wise NTK-bounding relaxation makes certificates tight for small budgets but loose for larger delta (a gap to the empirical APGD upper bound), even though the NTK bounds themselves are tight (Thm 3.5). Scalability: the MILP is NP-hard in general and only scales to common benchmark graphs (Cora-ML, WikiCS); it does not reach large-scale datasets, and cost grows with the number of labeled nodes. Scope: only node-FEATURE clean-label perturbations are certified, not graph-structure/edge poisoning (left as open, technically harder) and not label-flipping. Model assumption: guarantees hold exactly only in the infinite-width limit; for finite width the NN-SVM equivalence (and thus the certificate) holds with high probability, with output gap O(ln h / sqrt(h)). The semi-verified threat model can be overly pessimistic when much data is unverified but the adversary controls only a small part. Main results use L=1 hidden layer and binary classification in the body (multi-class/inductive in appendix).

datasets_used: Cora-ML (Bojchevski & Gunnemann 2018) with 384-dim abstract embeddings from the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 sentence transformer; 20 train nodes/class, 2925 unlabeled
Cora-MLb (binary subgraph of the two largest Cora-ML classes); 10 train nodes/class, 1215 unlabeled
WikiCSb (binary subgraph of the two largest WikiCS classes, Mernyei & Cangea 2022); 10 train nodes/class, 4640 unlabeled
Contextual Stochastic Block Model (CSBM, Deshpande et al. 2018) synthetic graphs; 200 nodes, 40 train nodes/class, 120 unlabeled
quantitative_results: In the Poison Labeled setting with padv=1 (all labeled nodes attackable), QPCert returns non-trivial (>0%) certified accuracy on Cora-MLb and WikiCSb, where a trivial certificate would give 0%
All evaluated GNNs achieve higher certified accuracy than an MLP across settings; most GNNs exceed the MLP baseline for budgets delta <= 0.1 in the Poison Unlabeled setting
Graph-connectivity inflection point at perturbation strength delta=0.05 for GCN on CSBM (higher connectivity helps for small delta, hurts for large delta)
APPNP teleport-parameter alpha inflection point at delta=0.1 on Cora-MLb PU (higher alpha helps provable robustness for large delta, hurts for small delta)
GIN and GraphSAGE consistently give the lowest certified accuracy among GNNs (attributed to sum-aggregation)
MILP size: (m+1)^2 + 5m variables with 2m binary, so runtime scales with number of labeled samples m
Finite-width NN vs SVM output gap bounded by O(ln h / sqrt(h)) for smallest hidden width h
Perturbation budgets swept delta in {0.01,0.02,0.05,0.1,0.2,0.5,1}; adversarial node fraction padv in {0.01,0.02,0.05,0.1,0.2,0.5,1}; p in {1,2,inf}
baselines_compared: MLP (graph-free NTK) as the main reference for isolating the value of graph structure (its training is unaffected by adversary in the PU setting)

No direct white-box poisoning-certificate baseline exists (this is the first); authors adapt the split-and-majority-vote black-box certificate of Levine & Feizi (2021) for node-feature perturbations for comparison (App. J.4)
Comparison against two common empirical poisoning defenses (App. J.8)
Concurrent white-box MLP poisoning certificate of Sosnin et al. (2024): QPCert certifies significantly larger budgets for MLPs (App. O.5)
Empirical attack upper bounds via APGD (Croce & Hein 2020) differentiated through training (QPLayer and MetaAttack surrogate) and the clean-label backdoor attack of Xing et al. (2024)
